## [00:00 - 03:02]

The ABCs of planning your course. Did you do your jumping jacks? Are you ready to dive into planning your course? Got your workbook ready? Good, because this lesson is where 80% of the work takes place. It's like an iceberg where most of it is below the water. Most of the work for a course is behind the scenes. Here's what people don't see about courses. This number may shock you, but for a one-hour course on mastermind.com, we put in probably 20 hours of prep time. That includes working with the instructor to decide on the angle of the course, laying out and reviewing the content, recording the content in the studio, editing the content several times, prepping supplemental materials like bonuses or worksheets, designing the handouts or other resources, and more. That's a lot, right? Now, that doesn't mean that you will have the same 20-to-1 formula, but the best courses do have a lot of prep work backing them up. Your students won't see the work you put in beforehand, but they will feel it. They'll notice it in the flow of your course. They'll notice in your comfort level in recording or presenting your content. They'll notice in the little things you add in, whether it's slides or handouts. In other words, they'll notice. Don't worry though, because we are going to walk through everything step by step to make sure your course is the best it can be. The first question to answer in your workbook before designing your course content is always, what do I really want people to do or think or believe as a result of taking my course? This is important because it's like a topic sentence of a paragraph. In a paragraph, all the other sentences support the topic sentence. In a course, all the other lessons or contents support that high-level outcome. If a particular point you want to make or an example you'd like to include or a story you'd like to tell doesn't support your ultimate goal, it needs to be cut. For example, for this course, my goal for you is, I want people to know how to easily create a course based on their irresistible product framework. I have a lot of sub-goals underneath that, like I want you to have fun and for you to feel confident about your content, but those are secondary to my primary goal. Everything I put in this portion of the Mastermind Business System needs to support that goal. If I wanted to include, say, some information on how to cut down on the number of emails you receive every day, that might be great, but it just doesn't fit. That's a dumb example, but you get my point. The goal for the course becomes the filter through which you evaluate the rest of your content. If you ever wonder, should I include this story or that tool or this example, just ask yourself, does it help my student achieve X, where X is your ultimate outcome you want your students to achieve? Let's do some examples. Let's say Joy's solution statement is, I'm a course creator who helps women to revamp their wardrobes to create a fashion forward look on a budget. She might say, as a result of taking my course, I want my students to know how to create a
